label: Product Requirements Doc
description: Write a sharp PRD with problem, scope, success metrics and open questions

fields.label: Product / feature name
fields.placeholder: Saved Views

fields.label: Problem & evidence
fields.placeholder: Power users re-filter the same lists daily; top support theme; ~30% of sessions.

fields.label: Goals & success metrics
fields.placeholder: Increase 4-week retention of power users by 5%; <2 clicks to a saved view.

fields.label: In scope (must-haves)
fields.placeholder: Create/rename/delete views, share with team, default view per user.

fields.label: Constraints & non-goals
fields.placeholder: No mobile in v1; must respect existing permissions; ship within the quarter.

label: Product Roadmap
description: Build an outcome-driven roadmap with themes, bets and sequencing

fields.label: Product / area
fields.placeholder: Analytics platform

fields.label: Vision & strategic context
fields.placeholder: Become the default analytics layer for mid-market; win on time-to-insight.

fields.label: Key objectives / outcomes
fields.placeholder: Grow activation 15%, cut TTFV to <1 day, land 3 enterprise logos.

fields.label: Candidate themes
fields.placeholder: Onboarding, collaboration, performance, governance, integrations.

fields.label: Constraints & known commitments
fields.placeholder: Team of 6; one committed enterprise integration; freeze in December.

label: User Stories
description: Break a feature into clear user stories with acceptance criteria

fields.label: Feature / epic name
fields.placeholder: Team sharing for saved views

fields.label: Context & goal
fields.placeholder: Users want to share saved views with teammates and control who can edit them.

fields.label: User personas involved
fields.placeholder: View owner, view collaborator, workspace admin.

fields.label: Constraints
fields.placeholder: Must respect existing RBAC; no schema migration in this slice; ship behind a flag.

template:
You are a product manager skilled at backlog refinement. Break down the feature "{{featureName}}" into user stories.

## Context
{{context}}
- Personas: {{personas}}
- Granularity: {{size}}

## How to write the stories
- Each story: "As a <persona>, I want <capability>, so that <value>" — independent, valuable, and testable.
- Acceptance criteria in {{acFormat}} format, specific and verifiable.
{{#if includeEdge}}- Include edge cases, error states, and non-functional stories (performance, accessibility, permissions).
{{/if}}{{#if size == "Epic → stories breakdown"}}- Present the epic, then a vertically-sliced set of stories that each ship independent value.
{{/if}}- Note dependencies and a sensible delivery order.

{{#if constraints}}## Constraints
{{constraints}}

{{/if}}## Deliverables
1. The ordered list of user stories with acceptance criteria.
2. A short note on slicing rationale and dependencies.
3. Any assumptions made and open questions worth flagging.

Write decisively with stated assumptions; ask only if genuinely blocked.

label: Release Notes
description: Turn a changelog into clear, benefit-led release notes for an audience

fields.label: Product name
fields.placeholder: Acme Analytics

fields.label: Raw changes / changelog
fields.placeholder: Added saved views; fixed CSV export timezone bug; faster dashboard load; deprecated old API v1.

fields.label: Constraints
fields.placeholder: Keep under 250 words; no internal jargon; link the migration guide for v1 API.

label: Discovery Interview Guide
description: Plan a bias-free customer discovery interview with sharp questions

fields.label: Research topic
fields.placeholder: How teams currently share analytics

fields.label: What you need to learn & decisions it informs
fields.placeholder: Whether sharing pain is real and frequent enough to prioritize; informs next-quarter bets.

fields.label: Hypotheses / assumptions to test
fields.placeholder: We assume sharing is done over Slack screenshots and that this loses context.

fields.label: Constraints
fields.placeholder: Non-technical participants; remote; avoid leading questions; record with consent.

template:
You are a product discovery / UX research expert. Create an interview guide on "{{topic}}".

## Objective
{{objective}}
- Interview type: {{interviewType}}
{{#if segment}}- Segment: {{segment}}
{{/if}}- Session length: {{duration}} minutes

## Hypotheses to test
{{hypotheses}}

## Guide to produce
- A brief intro script (purpose, consent, no-wrong-answers framing).
- Warm-up questions, then open, non-leading questions that explore past behavior over hypotheticals.
- Probing follow-ups ("tell me about the last time...", "why did that matter?") tied to each hypothesis.
- A timeboxed flow that fits {{duration}} minutes with priority questions marked.
- A wrap-up and what to capture immediately after.

{{#if constraints}}## Constraints
{{constraints}}

{{/if}}## Deliverables
1. The full interview guide with timings and priority markers.
2. A note on which hypothesis each question line attacks.
3. A simple synthesis template to capture findings consistently.

Design questions to avoid bias and leading; ask only if genuinely blocked.

label: Competitive Analysis
description: Produce a structured competitive analysis with positioning and gaps

fields.label: Our product
fields.placeholder: Acme Analytics

fields.label: Our positioning & target user
fields.placeholder: Self-serve analytics for mid-market ops teams; wins on speed and ease.

fields.label: Competitors to analyze
fields.placeholder: Looker, Metabase, Mixpanel, plus the 'spreadsheet' status quo.

fields.label: Constraints & known intel
fields.placeholder: Use only public info; competitor X just raised prices; we can't match their integrations.

template:
You are a product strategist. Produce a competitive analysis for "{{productName}}".

## Our context
{{ourContext}}
- Audience for this analysis: {{audience}}

## Competitors
{{competitors}}

## Analysis to produce
- A comparison across these dimensions: {{dimensions}}, with an honest, evidence-based assessment.
- A positioning map showing where we and each competitor sit and why.
- Our differentiators, our genuine weaknesses, and unmet needs / market gaps.
{{#if includeStrategy}}- Strategic recommendations: where to compete, where not to, and the sharpest wedge.
{{/if}}- Frame conclusions for the {{audience}} audience.

{{#if constraints}}## Constraints & known intel
{{constraints}}

{{/if}}## Deliverables
1. A comparison matrix across the chosen dimensions.
2. The positioning narrative with differentiators and gaps.
3. A prioritized set of takeaways and the assumptions behind them.

Be candid and avoid wishful thinking; ask only if genuinely blocked.

label: Feature Spec
description: Write a detailed feature spec covering behavior, states and edge cases

fields.label: Feature name
fields.placeholder: Bulk export

fields.label: What it does & why
fields.placeholder: Let users export filtered records to CSV/Excel asynchronously and get notified when ready.

fields.label: Primary user flow
fields.placeholder: User filters list → clicks Export → picks format → job runs → email + in-app link to download.

fields.label: Constraints & dependencies
fields.placeholder: Reuse existing job queue; max 100k rows; respects row-level permissions.

template:
You are a senior product manager writing an implementation-ready feature spec for "{{featureName}}".

## Summary
{{summary}}
- Surface: {{surface}}

## Primary flow
{{userFlow}}

## Spec to produce
- Detailed behavior for the happy path plus all branches and transitions.
- Cover these areas explicitly: {{details}}.
{{#if includeMocks}}- Describe each screen/interaction at mock level: layout intent, controls, copy, and state changes.
{{/if}}- Acceptance criteria that are precise and testable.
- Telemetry: the events/properties needed to measure success.

{{#if constraints}}## Constraints & dependencies
{{constraints}}

{{/if}}## Deliverables
1. The full spec in Markdown with the sections above.
2. A state/edge-case table (state → expected behavior).
3. Open questions and explicit assumptions, plus a suggested v1 cut line.

Write decisively with stated assumptions; ask only if genuinely blocked.

label: OKR Plan
description: Draft outcome-focused objectives and measurable key results for a team

fields.label: Team / area
fields.placeholder: Growth squad

fields.label: Strategic context & mission
fields.placeholder: Own activation; company goal is to double self-serve revenue this year.

fields.label: Focus areas / candidate objectives
fields.placeholder: Improve onboarding completion, reduce time-to-value, lift trial-to-paid conversion.

fields.label: Current baselines & known data
fields.placeholder: Onboarding completion 42%, TTFV 3.5 days, trial→paid 6.2%.

fields.label: Map initiatives to each key result
Constraints
fields.placeholder: Max 3 objectives; KRs must be outcome metrics not output; team of 5.

template:
You are a product operations leader skilled in OKRs. Draft a {{period}} OKR plan for "{{teamName}}".

## Strategic context
{{context}}
- Ambition level: {{ambition}}

## Focus areas
{{focus}}

## Baselines
{{baselines}}

## OKRs to produce
- A small set of inspiring, qualitative objectives that ladder up to the strategic context.
- For each objective, 2-4 key results that are measurable outcomes (not tasks), each with a baseline and a target.
- Avoid output/vanity metrics; make each KR independently verifiable and time-bound to the {{period}} period.
{{#if includeInitiatives}}- Map candidate initiatives to the key results they are bet to move, noting that initiatives are not KRs.
{{/if}}- Flag any KR that lacks a credible baseline or measurement method.

{{#if constraints}}## Constraints
{{constraints}}

{{/if}}## Deliverables
1. The full OKR set with baselines, targets, and measurement notes.
2. The initiative-to-KR mapping and what was deliberately excluded.
3. Risks to achievability and the assumptions the targets depend on.

Write decisively with stated assumptions; ask only if genuinely blocked.

label: User Persona
description: Build an evidence-grounded user persona that drives product decisions

fields.label: Persona name / label
fields.placeholder: Operations Olivia

fields.label: Segment & role this represents
fields.placeholder: Ops manager at a 50-200 person company who owns reporting but isn't technical.

fields.label: Research / evidence behind it
fields.placeholder: 12 interviews, support themes, product analytics on the power-user cohort.

fields.label: Constraints
fields.placeholder: Keep to one page; no invented demographics; mark low-confidence inferences.

template:
You are a UX researcher building a decision-driving persona: "{{personaName}}".

## Who this represents
{{segment}}
- Persona type: {{personaType}}

## Evidence base
{{research}}

## Persona to produce
- Ground every claim in the stated evidence; clearly mark inferences and low-confidence items rather than inventing detail.
- Include these sections: {{sections}}, focused on behavior and goals over decorative demographics.
- Make it specific enough to settle real design and prioritization debates.
{{#if includeImplications}}- Add explicit product/design implications: what this persona means we should and should not build.
{{/if}}{{#if personaType == "Anti-persona (not for)"}}- Be clear about why this user is explicitly out of scope and the trade-off that accepts.
{{/if}}
{{#if constraints}}## Constraints
{{constraints}}

{{/if}}## Deliverables
1. The one-page persona with the chosen sections.
2. The evidence-to-claim mapping and confidence flags.
3. The top decisions this persona should change and open research gaps.

Be evidence-led, not stereotype-led; ask only if genuinely blocked.

label: Customer Journey Map
description: Map a customer journey with stages, emotions, pain points and moments

fields.label: Journey / scenario
fields.placeholder: New team adopting the product from signup to first shared report

fields.label: Persona taking the journey
fields.placeholder: Ops manager evaluating tools for the team; non-technical, time-poor.

fields.label: Start and end points
fields.placeholder: Starts at ad click; ends when the team relies on it weekly (habit formed).

fields.label: Constraints
fields.placeholder: Base on known research only; flag assumptions; B2B multi-stakeholder journey.

template:
You are a service designer mapping the customer journey for "{{scenario}}".

## Persona
{{persona}}
- Primary lens: {{lens}}

## Scope
{{scope}}

## Map to produce
- Break the journey into clear, named stages across the defined start and end points.
- For each stage capture these lanes: {{lanes}}, keeping actions concrete and emotions tied to a specific cause.
- Highlight moments of truth, drop-off risks, and the single biggest friction point per stage.
{{#if includeOpportunities}}- Translate the worst pain points into prioritized opportunities (impact vs. effort) with an owner hint.
{{/if}}- Mark any stage based on assumption rather than evidence so it can be validated.

{{#if constraints}}## Constraints
{{constraints}}

{{/if}}## Deliverables
1. The stage-by-stage journey map as a table (stage x lanes).
2. The emotion curve summary and the top 3 moments that make or break it.
3. Prioritized opportunities and the assumptions to validate.

Be specific and evidence-aware; ask only if genuinely blocked.
